metadata.title: Why Claude can't think silently — reasoning only counts when it's on the page
metadata.topic: Reasoning has to be emitted to exist
metadata.purpose: A model has no hidden scratchpad — computation rides on generated tokens, so reasoning must be produced out loud (in tags) before the answer to actually influence it.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.still_lanes: c2v (two reasoning paths from one input)
metadata.prerequisites: basic prompting, tokens as generated output
metadata.visual_object: One review with two output paths — "answer only" (blank thought bubble, wrong verdict) vs "brainstorm-then-answer" (filled scratchpad, right verdict)
metadata.hook: Telling Claude to "think it through but only show the answer" produces no thinking at all — the reasoning has to be written as tokens or it never happens.

beats.narration_text: A two-sentence review whose second line reverses the first. Asked for a verdict directly, Claude takes "unrelated" too literally and answers wrong. Told to reason first inside `<brainstorm>` tags, it works through both sentences and flips to the correct verdict.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: Time-to-think should improve accuracy; asking Claude to think but output only the answer did not; why?
Telling Claude to "think it through but only show the answer" produces no thinking at all — the reasoning has to be written as tokens or it never happens.
A model has no hidden scratchpad — computation rides on generated tokens, so reasoning must be produced out loud (in tags) before the answer to actually influence it.
beats.visual: One review with two output paths — "answer only" (blank thought bubble, wrong verdict) vs "brainstorm-then-answer" (filled scratchpad, right verdict)
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: Review: "The food arrived cold. Unrelated: it was the best meal of my life." Direct → "negative" (wrong); brainstorm-first → "positive".
A model has no hidden scratchpad — computation rides on generated tokens, so reasoning must be produced out loud (in tags) before the answer to actually influence it.
